subject: Physics
q: The terminal velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>t</mi></msub></mfenced></math> of the spherical rain drop depends on the radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>r</mi></mfenced></math> of the spherical rain drop as
question: The terminal velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>t</mi></msub></mfenced></math> of the spherical rain drop depends on the radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>r</mi></mfenced></math> of the spherical rain drop as
solution:
The terminal velocity of a sphere of radius r and density ρ, immersed in a liquid of density σ and viscosity η is given by vT=29ρ-σr2gη

Hence, for spherical raindrop, vT∝r2.

subject: Physics
q: If two glass plates have water between them and are separated by very small distance (see figure), it is very difficult to pull them apart. It is because the water in between forms cylindrical surface on the side that gives rise to lower pressure in the water in comparison to atmosphere. If the radius of the cylindrical surface is R and surface tension of water is T then the pressure in water between the plates is lower by:
question: If two glass plates have water between them and are separated by very small distance (see figure), it is very difficult to pull them apart. It is because the water in between forms cylindrical surface on the side that gives rise to lower pressure in the water in comparison to atmosphere. If the radius of the cylindrical surface is R and surface tension of water is T then the pressure in water between the plates is lower by:
solution:
d=2R cosθ

∴    Pressure difference across a liquid surface is
∆P=T 1R1+1R2

∵   R1=R and R2=R

∆P=T1R+1R

∆P= 2TR

∴     Pressure is more in the concave side hence pressure in water between the plates is lower by 2TR

subject: Physics
q:
An open glass tube is immersed in mercury in such a way that a length of 8 cm extends above the mercury level. The open end of the tube is then closed and sealed and the tube is raised vertically up by additional 46 cm. What will be length of the air column above mercury in the tube now ?
(Atmospheric pressure = 76 cm of Hg)
question:
An open glass tube is immersed in mercury in such a way that a length of 8 cm extends above the mercury level. The open end of the tube is then closed and sealed and the tube is raised vertically up by additional 46 cm. What will be length of the air column above mercury in the tube now ?
(Atmospheric pressure = 76 cm of Hg)
solution:
Using isothermal process conditions



⇒ P 1 V 1 = P 2 V 2 ⇒ P 1 A ℓ 1 = P 2 A ℓ 2

8 p 0 = xp ⇒ p = 8 p 0 x     (here p0 = 76 cm of Hg)

8 p 0 x + ρ m g 54 - x = ρ m g (76)

8 x + 54 - x 76 = 1

x = 16 cm

subject: Physics
q: A small spherical droplet of density <math><mi>d</mi></math> is floating exactly half immersed in a liquid of density <math><mi>ρ</mi></math> and surface tension <math><mi>T</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The radius of the droplet is (take note that the surface tension applies an upward force on the droplet):
question: A small spherical droplet of density <math><mi>d</mi></math> is floating exactly half immersed in a liquid of density <math><mi>ρ</mi></math> and surface tension <math><mi>T</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The radius of the droplet is (take note that the surface tension applies an upward force on the droplet):
solution:
dVg=pV2g+T2πr
⇒d.43πr3g=p.23πr3g+2πrT
⇒23r2g2d-p=2T
⇒r=3T2d-pg

subject: Physics
q: An air bubble of diameter <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> rises steadily through a solution of density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1750</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> at the rate of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>35</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The co-efficient of viscosity of the solution (neglect density of air) is ________ Pas (given, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>).
question: An air bubble of diameter <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> rises steadily through a solution of density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1750</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> at the rate of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>35</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The co-efficient of viscosity of the solution (neglect density of air) is ________ Pas (given, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>).
solution:
The bubble is moving with a constant velocity so the net force acting on the body is zero.

Stokes' law defines the drag force which is given by F=6πηrv

The buoyant force is given by B=Vρg=43πr3ρg

Hence, it can be written that

43πr3ρg=6πηrv⇒η=2r2ρg9v=2×3×10-32×1750×109×0.35×10-2=10 Pas

subject: Physics
q: Consider a water tank as shown in the figure. It's cross-sectional area is 0.4 m2. The tank has an opening B near the bottom whose cross-section area is 1 cm2. A load of 24 kg is applied on the water at the top when the height of the water level is 40 cm above the bottom, the velocity of water coming out the opening B is v m s-1. The value of v, to the nearest integer, is ___ .[Take the value of g to be 10 m s-2]
question: Consider a water tank as shown in the figure. It's cross-sectional area is 0.4 m2. The tank has an opening B near the bottom whose cross-section area is 1 cm2. A load of 24 kg is applied on the water at the top when the height of the water level is 40 cm above the bottom, the velocity of water coming out the opening B is v m s-1. The value of v, to the nearest integer, is ___ .[Take the value of g to be 10 m s-2]
solution:
m=24 kg

A=0.4 m2

a=1 cm2

H=40 cm

Using Bernoulli's equation, 

⇒P0+mgA+ρgH+12Pv12

=P0+0+12ρv2   ...1

⇒ Neglecting v1

⇒v=2gH+2mgAρ

⇒v=8+1.2

⇒v=3.033 m s-1

⇒v≃3 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: The ratio of surface tensions of mercury and water is given to be <math><mn>7.5</mn></math>, while the ratio of their densities is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>13.6</mn></math>. Their contact angles, with glass, are close to <math><mn>135</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo></math>, respectively. If it is observed that mercury gets depressed by an amount <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> in a capillary tube of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>, while water rises by the same amount <math><mi>h</mi></math> in a capillary tube of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, then the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>r</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> is close to
question: The ratio of surface tensions of mercury and water is given to be <math><mn>7.5</mn></math>, while the ratio of their densities is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>13.6</mn></math>. Their contact angles, with glass, are close to <math><mn>135</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo></math>, respectively. If it is observed that mercury gets depressed by an amount <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> in a capillary tube of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>, while water rises by the same amount <math><mi>h</mi></math> in a capillary tube of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, then the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>r</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> is close to
solution:
|hHg|=|hwater|

2S1cosθ1ρ1r1g=2S2cosθ2ρ2r2g

Therefore, the ratio of the radii of the respective tubes is,

r1r2=ρ2ρ1×S1cosθ1S2cosθ2

r1r2=113.6×7.5×12

r1r2≈25

subject: Physics
q: Water flows m a horizontal tube (see figure). The pressure of water changes by 700 Nm-2 between A and B where the area of cross section are 40 cm2 and 20 cm2, respectively. Find the rate of flow of water through the tube. (density of water =1000 kgm-3 )
question: Water flows m a horizontal tube (see figure). The pressure of water changes by 700 Nm-2 between A and B where the area of cross section are 40 cm2 and 20 cm2, respectively. Find the rate of flow of water through the tube. (density of water =1000 kgm-3 )
solution:
Using equation of continuity
40VA=20VB
⇒2VA=VB
Using Bernoulli’s equation
PA+12ρVA2=PB+12ρVB2
⇒PA-PB=12VB2-VA2
⇒ΔP=121000VB2-VB24
⇒ΔP=500×3VB24
⇒VB=ΔP×41500=700×41500ms
Volume flow rate =20×100×VB=2732 cm3/s

subject: Physics
q: A liquid of density $\rho$ is coming out of a hose pipe of radius a with horizontal speed $v$ and hits a mesh. $50 \%$ of the liquid passes through the mesh unaffected. $25 \%$ looses all of its momentum and $25 \%$ comes back with the same speed. The resultant pressure on the mesh will be:
question: A liquid of density $\rho$ is coming out of a hose pipe of radius a with horizontal speed $v$ and hits a mesh. $50 \%$ of the liquid passes through the mesh unaffected. $25 \%$ looses all of its momentum and $25 \%$ comes back with the same speed. The resultant pressure on the mesh will be:
solution:
Mass per unit time of the liquid = \rhoav Momentum per second carried by liquid $=\rho a v \times v$

Net force due to bounced back liquid, $\mathrm{F}_{1}=2 \times\left[\frac{1}{4} \rho \mathrm{av}^{2}\right]$

Net force due to stopped liquid, $\mathrm{F}_{2}=\frac{1}{4}$ pav $^{2}$ Total force,

$\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{F}_{1}+\mathrm{F}_{2}=\frac{1}{2} \rho \mathrm{av}^{2}+\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{pav}^{2}=\frac{3}{4} \mathrm{pav}^{2}$

Net pressure $=\frac{3}{4} \rho v^{2}$

subject: Physics
q: In an experiment, a small steel ball falls through a liquid at a constant speed of $10 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$. If the steel ball is pulled upward with a force equal to twice its effective weight, how fast will it move upward ?
question: In an experiment, a small steel ball falls through a liquid at a constant speed of $10 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$. If the steel ball is pulled upward with a force equal to twice its effective weight, how fast will it move upward ?
solution: Weight of the body $\mathrm{W}=\mathrm{mg}=\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{r}^3 \rho \mathrm{g}$ $\mathrm{T}=\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{r}^3 \sigma \mathrm{g}$ and $\mathrm{F}=6 \pi \eta \mathrm{vr}$ When the body attains terminal velocity net force acting on the body is zero. i.e., $\mathrm{W}-\mathrm{T}-\mathrm{F}=0$ And terminal velocity $\mathrm{v}=\frac{2}{9} \frac{\mathrm{r}^2(\rho-\sigma) \mathrm{g}}{\eta}$ As in case of upward motion upward force is twice its effective weight, therefore, it will move with same speed $10 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$

subject: Physics
q:
On heating water, bubbles being formed at the bottom of the vessel detatch and rise. Take the bubbles to be spheres of radius R and making a circular contact of radius r with the bottom of the vessel. If r << R, and the surface tension of water is T, value of r just before bubbles detatch is :
(density of water is ρ w )
question:
On heating water, bubbles being formed at the bottom of the vessel detatch and rise. Take the bubbles to be spheres of radius R and making a circular contact of radius r with the bottom of the vessel. If r << R, and the surface tension of water is T, value of r just before bubbles detatch is :
(density of water is ρ w )
solution:
When the bubble gets detached,

Buoyant force = force due to surface tension


∫ T × dl sin θ = 4 3 π R 3 ρ w g

⇒ T × 2 π r × r R = 4 3 π R 3 ρ w g
⇒ r 2 = 2 R 4 ρ w g 3T

subject: Physics
q:
A fully loaded boeing aircraft has a mass of 5.4×105 kg. Its total wing area is 500 m2. It is in level flight with a speed of 1080 km h-1. If the density of air ρ is 1.2 kg m–3, the fractional increase in the speed of the air on the upper surface of the wing relative to the lower surface in percentage will be

(g=10 m s-2)
question:
A fully loaded boeing aircraft has a mass of 5.4×105 kg. Its total wing area is 500 m2. It is in level flight with a speed of 1080 km h-1. If the density of air ρ is 1.2 kg m–3, the fractional increase in the speed of the air on the upper surface of the wing relative to the lower surface in percentage will be

(g=10 m s-2)
solution:
Required lift force, F=P2A-P1A=5.4×105×g

⇒P2-P1=5.4×106500=5.4×2×103

=10.8×103

Now applying Bernoulli's Theorem we get

P2+0+12ρV22=P1+0+12ρV12

⇒P2-P1=12ρV12-V22=12ρV1-V2V1+V2Since for small change, V1=V2=V=1080 km h-1=300 m s-1

⇒10.8×103=12×1.2V1-V2×2×300

⇒10.8×10=3.6V1-V2

⇒V1-V2=30

Therefore, fractional increase will beV1-V2V×100=30300×100=10%

subject: Physics
q: When an air bubble of radius $r$ rises from the bottom to the surface of a lake, its radius becomes $\frac{5 \mathrm{r}}{4}$. Taking the atmospheric pressure to be equal to $10 \mathrm{~m}$ height of water column, the depth of the lake would approximately be (ignore the surface tension and the effect of temperature):
question: When an air bubble of radius $r$ rises from the bottom to the surface of a lake, its radius becomes $\frac{5 \mathrm{r}}{4}$. Taking the atmospheric pressure to be equal to $10 \mathrm{~m}$ height of water column, the depth of the lake would approximately be (ignore the surface tension and the effect of temperature):
solution:
Using $\mathrm{P}_1 \mathrm{~V}_1=\mathrm{P}_2 \mathrm{~V}_2$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\left(P_1\right) \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3=\left(P_2\right) \frac{4}{3} \pi \frac{125 r^3}{64} \\
&\frac{\rho g(10)+\rho g h}{\rho g(10)}=\frac{125}{64} \\
&640+64 h=1250
\end{aligned}
$$
On solving we get $h=9.5 \mathrm{~m}$

question: The amount of work done to break a big water drop of radius ' $R$ ' into 27 small drops of equal radius is 10 J . The work done required to break the same big drop into 64 small drops of equal radius will be

subject: Physics
q: Two identical cylindrical vessels are kept on the ground and each contain the same liquid of density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math>. The area of the base of both vessels is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> but the height of liquid in one vessel is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and in the other <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. When both cylinders are connected through a pipe of negligible volume very close to the bottom, the liquid flows from one vessel to the other until it comes to equilibrium at a new height. The change in energy of the system in the process is :
question: Two identical cylindrical vessels are kept on the ground and each contain the same liquid of density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math>. The area of the base of both vessels is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> but the height of liquid in one vessel is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and in the other <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. When both cylinders are connected through a pipe of negligible volume very close to the bottom, the liquid flows from one vessel to the other until it comes to equilibrium at a new height. The change in energy of the system in the process is :
solution:
Initial height of liquid in container's of same cross section are x1 and x2  respectively. Now value is opened find loss in potential energy when water level be become same

loss in PE=Ui-Uf

=ρ(A)x1x12+ρAx2x22g-ρAx1+x22×x1+x24×2g

=ρAgx122+x222-x1+x124=ρAgx1-x224

subject: Physics
q:
Water from a tap emerges vertically downwards with an initial speed of  1.0 ms-1 . The cross - sectional area of the tap is  10-4 m2 . Assume that the pressure is constant throughout the stream of water and that the flow is streamlined. The cross - sectional area of the stream, 0.15 m below the tap would be:
(Take  g=10 ms-2 )
question:
Water from a tap emerges vertically downwards with an initial speed of  1.0 ms-1 . The cross - sectional area of the tap is  10-4 m2 . Assume that the pressure is constant throughout the stream of water and that the flow is streamlined. The cross - sectional area of the stream, 0.15 m below the tap would be:
(Take  g=10 ms-2 )
solution:
Velocity of water after falling through distance 0.15 m is
v2=u2+2as
=1+2×10×0.15
v2=4
v=2 m/s
From equation of continuity
A1v1=A2v2
A2=10-4×12=5×10-5 m2

subject: Physics
q:
This question has Statement-1 and Statement- 2 . Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statetnents.
Statement-1: A capillary is dipped in a liquid and liquid rises to a height $h$ in it. As the temperature of the liquid is raised, the height $h$ increases (if the density of the liquid and the angle of contact remain the same).
Statement-2: Surface tension of a liquid decreases with the rise in its temperature.
question:
This question has Statement-1 and Statement- 2 . Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statetnents.
Statement-1: A capillary is dipped in a liquid and liquid rises to a height $h$ in it. As the temperature of the liquid is raised, the height $h$ increases (if the density of the liquid and the angle of contact remain the same).
Statement-2: Surface tension of a liquid decreases with the rise in its temperature.
options: Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true; Statement-2 is not the correct explanation for Statement-1.
Statement-1 is false, Statement- 2 is true.
Statement-1 is true, Statement- 2 is false.
Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true; Statement-2 is the correct explanation for Statement-1.
solution:
Surface tension of a liquid decreases with the rise in temperture. At the boiling point of liquid, surface tension is zero.
Capillary rise $h=\frac{2 \mathrm{~T} \cos \theta}{\mathrm{rdg}}$
As surface tension $\mathrm{T}$ decreases with rise in temperature hence capillary rise also decreases.

subject: Physics
q: A cylinder of height $20 \mathrm{~m}$ is completely filled with water. The velocity of efflux of water (in $\mathrm{ms}^{-1}$ ) through a small hole on the side wall of the cylinder near its bottom is
question: A cylinder of height $20 \mathrm{~m}$ is completely filled with water. The velocity of efflux of water (in $\mathrm{ms}^{-1}$ ) through a small hole on the side wall of the cylinder near its bottom is
solution: $v=\sqrt{2 g h}=\sqrt{2 \times 10 \times 20}=20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

subject: Physics
q: Two soap bubbles of radius 2 cm and 4 cm , respectively, are in contact with each other. The radius of curvature of the common surface, in cm , is ______ .
question: Two soap bubbles of radius 2 cm and 4 cm , respectively, are in contact with each other. The radius of curvature of the common surface, in cm , is ______ .
solution: $R=\frac{R_2 R_1}{R_2-R_1}=\frac{4 \times 2}{2}=4 \mathrm{~cm}$

subject: Physics
q: A large number of water drops, each of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math>, combine to have a drop of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>. If the surface tension is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> and mechanical equivalent of heat is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>J</mi></math>, the rise in heat energy per unit volume will be:
question: A large number of water drops, each of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math>, combine to have a drop of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>. If the surface tension is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> and mechanical equivalent of heat is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>J</mi></math>, the rise in heat energy per unit volume will be:
solution:
n×43πr3=43πR3

∴ n1/3r=R

∴ Total change in surface energy

=n4πr2-4πR2T

⇒4πTnr2-R2

∴ Heat energy

=4πTnr2-R2J×43πR3=3 T Jnr2R3-1R

Put nr3=R3

∴ 3TJ1r-1R

subject: Physics
q: An air bubble of radius 0.1 cm lies at a depth of 20 cm below the free surface of a liquid of density $1000 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$. If the pressure inside the bubble is $2100 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$ greater than the atmospheric pressure, then the surface tension of the liquid in SI unit is (use $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ )
question: An air bubble of radius 0.1 cm lies at a depth of 20 cm below the free surface of a liquid of density $1000 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$. If the pressure inside the bubble is $2100 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$ greater than the atmospheric pressure, then the surface tension of the liquid in SI unit is (use $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ )
solution: $\begin{aligned} & P_1=P_0+\rho g h=P_0+1000 \times 10 \times \frac{20}{100} \\ & \Rightarrow P_1=P_0+2000 \end{aligned}$ So, $P_2-P_1=\frac{2 S}{R}=\left(\frac{2 S}{1 \times 10^{-3}}\right)$ $\Rightarrow P_2=P_0+2100$ (given) $\begin{aligned} & \text { So, } P_0+2100-P_0-2000=2 S \times 10^3 \\ & \Rightarrow 100=2 S \times 10^3 \\ & \Rightarrow s=\left(\frac{1}{20}\right)=0.05 \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A submarine experiences a pressure of <math><mn>5.05</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></math> at a depth of <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> in a sea. When it goes further to a depth of <math><mi> </mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> , it experiences a pressure of <math><mi> </mi><mn>8.08</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></math> . Then <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> is approximately (density of water <math><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mrow></math> and acceleration due to gravity <math><mi> </mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> ):
question: A submarine experiences a pressure of <math><mn>5.05</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></math> at a depth of <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> in a sea. When it goes further to a depth of <math><mi> </mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> , it experiences a pressure of <math><mi> </mi><mn>8.08</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></math> . Then <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> is approximately (density of water <math><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mrow></math> and acceleration due to gravity <math><mi> </mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> ):
solution:
The pressure at a depth d is p=p0+ρgd, here p0 is the atmospheric pressure.
∴p1=p0+ρgd1
and p2=p0+ρgd2
The difference in pressure is p2-p1=ρgd2-d1

Putting the values in the above equation
8.08×106-5.05×106=103×10d2-d1
d2-d1=3.03×106104
=303 m

subject: Physics
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>64</mn></math> identical drops each charged upto potential of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mV</mi></math> are combined to form a bigger drop. The potential of the bigger drop will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mV</mi></math>.
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>64</mn></math> identical drops each charged upto potential of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mV</mi></math> are combined to form a bigger drop. The potential of the bigger drop will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mV</mi></math>.
solution:
The formula to calculate the potential V of each tiny drop is given by

V=kqr   ...1

When all the drops forms the bigger drop the radius of the new drop r' can be calculated as follows:

Volume of big drop=64×volume of each tiny drop⇒43πr'3=64×43πr3⇒r'=4r

So, the potential of the new big drop V' can be written as

V'=k64qr'=k64q4r=16kqr   ...2

Divide equation (2) by equation (1) and simplify to obtain the potential of the new drop.

V'V=16kqrkqr⇒V'=16V   ...3

Substitute the value of the known parameter into equation (3) to calculate the required value of the potential for the new drop.

V'=16×10 mV=160 mV

subject: Physics
q:
The figure shows a liquid of given density flowing steadily in horizontal tube of varying cross-section. Cross-sectional areas at A is 1.5 cm2, and B is 25 mm2, if the speed of liquid at B is 60 cm s-1 then (PA–PB) is

(Given PA and PB are liquid pressures at A and B points.

Density ρ=1000 kg m-3

A and B are on the axis of tube)
question:
The figure shows a liquid of given density flowing steadily in horizontal tube of varying cross-section. Cross-sectional areas at A is 1.5 cm2, and B is 25 mm2, if the speed of liquid at B is 60 cm s-1 then (PA–PB) is

(Given PA and PB are liquid pressures at A and B points.

Density ρ=1000 kg m-3

A and B are on the axis of tube)
solution:
Using Bernoulli's theorem,

PA+12ρVA2=PB+12ρVB2   ...(i)

Using equation of continuity,

AAVA=ABVB

So, 1.5×10-4×VA=25×10-6×VB

VA=25×10-61.5×10-4×0.6

=110 m s-1

Substituting in equation (i)

⇒PA-PB=100020.62-0.12=10002×0.7×0.5

=175 Pa

subject: Physics
q:
A 400 g solid cube having an edge of length 10 cm floats in water. How much volume of the cube is outside the water ?
(Given : density of water $=1000 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^{-3}$ )
question:
A 400 g solid cube having an edge of length 10 cm floats in water. How much volume of the cube is outside the water ?
(Given : density of water $=1000 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^{-3}$ )
solution:
Volume of cube inside water
$\begin{aligned}
& =\left(\frac{\text { Denslty of cube }}{\text { Denslty of water }}\right) \times \text { Volume of cube } \\
& =\frac{\text { Mass of cube }}{\text { Denslty of water }} \\
& =\frac{400 \mathrm{gm}}{1 \mathrm{gm} / \mathrm{cm}^3} \\
& =400 \mathrm{~cm}^3
\end{aligned}$
Volume of cube outside water
$\begin{aligned}
& =\text { Volume of cube }- \text { Volume of cube inside water } \\
& =1000 \mathrm{~cm}^3-400 \mathrm{~cm}^3 \\
& =600 \mathrm{~cm}^3
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A thin liquid film formed between a U-shaped wire and a light slider supports a weight of $1.5 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~N}$ (see figure). The length of the slider is $30 \mathrm{~cm}$ and its weight negligible. The surface tension of the liquid film is
question: A thin liquid film formed between a U-shaped wire and a light slider supports a weight of $1.5 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~N}$ (see figure). The length of the slider is $30 \mathrm{~cm}$ and its weight negligible. The surface tension of the liquid film is
solution: The force of surface tension acting on the slider balances the force due to the weight. $\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & \mathrm{F}=2 \mathrm{~T} \ell=\mathrm{w} \\ \Rightarrow & 2 \mathrm{~T}(0.3)=1.5 \times 10^{-2} \\ \Rightarrow & \mathrm{T}=2.5 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m} \end{array}$

subject: Physics
q: An air bubble of radius 1.0 mm is observed at a depth of 20 cm below the free surface of a liquid having surface tension $0.095 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{m}^2$ and density $10^3 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$. The difference between pressure inside the bubble and atmospheric pressure is _____ $\mathrm{N} / \mathrm{m}^2$. (Take $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ )
question: An air bubble of radius 1.0 mm is observed at a depth of 20 cm below the free surface of a liquid having surface tension $0.095 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{m}^2$ and density $10^3 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$. The difference between pressure inside the bubble and atmospheric pressure is _____ $\mathrm{N} / \mathrm{m}^2$. (Take $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ )
solution: $\Delta \mathrm{P}=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{in}}-\mathrm{P}_0$ $\begin{aligned} & =\rho \mathrm{gh}+\frac{2 \mathrm{~T}}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{1000 \times 10 \times 20}{100}+\frac{2 \times 0.095}{10^{-3}} \\ & =2000+190 \\ & =2190\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The height of liquid column raised in a capillary tube of certain radius when dipped in liquid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> vertically is, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. If the tube is dipped in a similar manner in another liquid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of surface tension and density double the values of liquid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>, the height of liquid column raised in liquid B would be ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
question: The height of liquid column raised in a capillary tube of certain radius when dipped in liquid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> vertically is, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. If the tube is dipped in a similar manner in another liquid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of surface tension and density double the values of liquid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>, the height of liquid column raised in liquid B would be ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
solution:
The height h through which a liquid will rise in a capillary tube of radius r is given by,

h=2Tcosθrρg∝Tρ∝2T2ρ

As we can see in both cases, value of height will remain same. Therefore,

h'=h=5 cm= 0.05 m

q: A small spherical ball of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math>, falling through a viscous medium of negligible density has terminal velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math>. Another ball of the same mass but of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>r</mi></math>, falling through the same viscous medium will have terminal velocity:
question: A small spherical ball of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math>, falling through a viscous medium of negligible density has terminal velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math>. Another ball of the same mass but of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>r</mi></math>, falling through the same viscous medium will have terminal velocity:
solution:
Ignoring buoyancy force, we can write terminal velocity as 

mg=6πηrv

⇒v∝1r (as mass is constant)

Now, vv'=r'r

As r'=2r

So v'=v2

q: Two small drops of mercury each of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> coalesce to form a single large drop. The ratio of total surface energy before and after the change is
question: Two small drops of mercury each of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> coalesce to form a single large drop. The ratio of total surface energy before and after the change is
solution:
43πR3+43πR3=43πR'3

R'=213R  …i

Ai=24πR2

Af=4πR'2

UiUf=AiAf=2R2223R2=213

subject: Physics
q: Water is flowing through a horizontal tube having cross-sectional areas of its two ends being $A$ and $A^{\prime}$ such that the ratio $A / A^{\prime}$ is 5 . If the pressure difference of water between the two ends is $3 \times$ $10^5 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$, the velocity of water with which it enters the tube will be (neglect gravity effects)
question: Water is flowing through a horizontal tube having cross-sectional areas of its two ends being $A$ and $A^{\prime}$ such that the ratio $A / A^{\prime}$ is 5 . If the pressure difference of water between the two ends is $3 \times$ $10^5 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$, the velocity of water with which it enters the tube will be (neglect gravity effects)
solution:
According to Bernoulli's theorem
$$
P_1+\frac{1}{2} \rho v_1^2=P_2+\frac{1}{2} \rho v_2^2 \ldots(\mathrm{i})
$$
From question,
$$
P_1-P_2=3 \times 10^5, \frac{A_1}{A_2}=5
$$
According to equation of continuity

$$
\begin{aligned}
& A_1 v_1=A_2 v_2 \\
& \text { or, } \frac{A_1}{A_2}=\frac{v_2}{v_1}=5 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad v_2=5 v_1
\end{aligned}
$$
From equation (i)
$$
\begin{aligned}
& P_1-P_2=\frac{1}{2} \rho\left(v_2^2-v_1^2\right) \\
& \text { or } 3 \times 10^5=\frac{1}{2} \times 1000\left(5 v_1^2-v_1^2\right. \\
& \Rightarrow \quad 600=6 v_1 \times 4 v_1 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad v_1^2=25 \\
& \therefore \quad v_1=5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: A spherical drop of liquid splits into <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn></math> identical spherical drops. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>u</mi><mi>i</mi></msub></math> is the surface energy of the original drop and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>u</mi><mi>f</mi></msub></math> is the total surface energy of the resulting drops, the (ignoring evaporation), <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi mathvariant="italic">u</mi><mi mathvariant="italic">f</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="italic">u</mi><mi mathvariant="italic">i</mi></msub></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mfrac><mn>10</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac></mfenced></math>. Then value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______:
question: A spherical drop of liquid splits into <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn></math> identical spherical drops. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>u</mi><mi>i</mi></msub></math> is the surface energy of the original drop and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>u</mi><mi>f</mi></msub></math> is the total surface energy of the resulting drops, the (ignoring evaporation), <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi mathvariant="italic">u</mi><mi mathvariant="italic">f</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="italic">u</mi><mi mathvariant="italic">i</mi></msub></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mfrac><mn>10</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac></mfenced></math>. Then value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______:
solution:
Let R be the radius of bigger drop and r be the radius of smaller drop.

Here, volume will remain conserved, so VR=Vr.

⇒43πR3=1000×43πr3

⇒R=10r

As surface energy is stated as u=SA, here, S is surface tension and A is surface area.

Thus, ui=S4πR2 and uf=S4πr2×1000

The ratio is ufui=1000r2R2

⇒ufui=101

Hence, the value of x=1.

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: One is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).

Assertion (A): Clothes containing oil or grease stains cannot be cleaned by water wash.

Reason (R) : Because the angle of contact between the oil/ grease and water is obtuse. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the option given below.
question:
Given below are two statements: One is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).

Assertion (A): Clothes containing oil or grease stains cannot be cleaned by water wash.

Reason (R) : Because the angle of contact between the oil/ grease and water is obtuse. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the option given below.
options: Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
Both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)
solution:
Angle of contact between oil and water is obtuse i.e., greater than critical angle of contact θc>90°, therefore they do not stick and that's the reason grease/oil stains can not be removed by water wash. 

Thus, both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A).
